content.text: Why Token and Cost Metrics Are Operational Signals

For traditional services, cost is a finance concern that surfaces monthly in a cloud bill. For GenAI services, cost is an operational signal that changes by the minute. A single misconfigured prompt template, a broken cache that forces re-embedding on every request, or a traffic spike from a bot can double the daily spend in hours. Token usage is simultaneously a cost driver, a performance indicator (high input token counts inflate latency), and a saturation predictor (token rate determines when you hit provider rate limits).

Teams that treat token and cost metrics as pure finance reporting miss the operational leverage. An anomaly in the input-to-output token ratio is often the first signal of a prompt bloat regression — visible in the token metrics minutes before latency or error rates move. A spend rate that is accelerating on a Tuesday afternoon when traffic is flat indicates a cost-per-request increase, which narrows the diagnosis to a model change, a prompt change, or a caching failure.
Cost as a canary:

A sudden 30 % increase in cost-per-request with stable traffic and stable latency is almost always caused by either a prompt template change that added tokens or a cache invalidation that stopped serving cached completions. These are diagnosable within minutes if cost-per-request is charted with a 5-minute resolution.

attrs.alt: Token usage and cost signal hierarchy for GenAI services

content.text: Raw Token Metrics to Emit

The foundation is accurate token counting from the provider response object. Never estimate tokens from a local tokenizer — provider token counts include special tokens and formatting overhead that local estimators miss, and the discrepancy accumulates into significant billing errors.

from opentelemetry import metrics
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


meter = metrics.get_meter("genai-service")

# --- Token counters ---
token_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_completion_tokens_total",
    description="Cumulative tokens consumed. Tag token_type=input|output to split.",
    unit="1",
)

# --- Cost counter ---
cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_cost_usd_total",
    description="Cumulative spend in USD. Tag model, provider, team, feature, tier.",
    unit="USD",
)

# --- Rate limit gauge ---
rate_limit_remaining = meter.create_gauge(
    "genai_provider_rate_limit_remaining_tokens",
    description="Remaining tokens in the current rate-limit window",
    unit="1",
)

# --- Context window utilisation histogram ---
context_utilisation = meter.create_histogram(
    "genai_chat_context_utilisation_ratio",
    description="prompt_tokens / max_context_tokens. Values near 1.0 risk truncation.",
    unit="1",
)


# Pricing table — update when providers change prices.
# Prices are per 1,000,000 tokens (input, output).
MODEL_PRICES: dict[str, tuple[float, float]] = {
    "gpt-4o":                     (5.00,  15.00),
    "gpt-4o-mini":                (0.15,   0.60),
    "gpt-4-turbo":                (10.00,  30.00),
    "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022": (3.00,  15.00),
    "claude-3-haiku-20240307":    (0.25,   1.25),
    "claude-opus-4":              (15.00,  75.00),
    "gemini-1.5-pro":             (3.50,  10.50),
    "gemini-1.5-flash":           (0.075,  0.30),
}


def compute_cost_usd(model: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    input_price, output_price = MODEL_PRICES.get(model, (5.00, 15.00))
    return (input_tokens * input_price + output_tokens * output_price) / 1_000_000


@dataclass
class TokenUsageRecord:
    model: str
    provider: str
    env: str
    service: str
    region: str
    team: str
    feature: str
    tier: str          # free | pro | enterprise
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    max_context_tokens: int

    @property
    def cost_usd(self) -> float:
        return compute_cost_usd(self.model, self.input_tokens, self.output_tokens)

    @property
    def context_utilisation(self) -> float:
        return self.input_tokens / self.max_context_tokens

    def emit_metrics(self) -> None:
        base = {
            "model": self.model, "provider": self.provider, "env": self.env,
            "service": self.service, "region": self.region,
            "team": self.team, "feature": self.feature, "tier": self.tier,
        }
        token_counter.add(self.input_tokens,  {**base, "token_type": "input"})
        token_counter.add(self.output_tokens, {**base, "token_type": "output"})
        cost_counter.add(self.cost_usd, base)
        context_utilisation.record(self.context_utilisation, base)
Derived Cost Signals
Raw counters answer "how much have we spent?" Derived metrics answer the operational question: "is our cost-per-request changing, and if so, why?"
Cost Per Output Token

Cost per output token normalises spend against the value delivered. If cost per token is rising while output quality is stable, a higher-priced model is being used or input tokens are increasing (since many providers price input and output separately). This metric is the primary cost efficiency indicator.

# Cost per 1K output tokens over the last 5 minutes
# Use in Grafana panels for real-time cost efficiency monitoring.

# Global cost per 1K output tokens
sum(rate(genai_cost_usd_total[5m]))
  /
(sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="output"}[5m])) / 1000)

# Segmented by model — reveals which model is most cost-efficient
sum by (model) (rate(genai_cost_usd_total[5m]))
  /
sum by (model) (
  rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="output"}[5m]) / 1000
)
Input-to-Output Token Ratio

The ratio of input tokens to output tokens is a prompt health signal. A ratio of 4:1 (4 input tokens per output token) is typical for a chat completion with a moderate system prompt. A spike to 20:1 indicates either a bloated system prompt, excessive RAG context injection, or a reduction in output length. A drop to 1:1 can indicate a prompt that is not providing enough context, leading to hallucinated output.

# Input:output token ratio — baseline in 24h window.
sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="input"}[5m]))
  /
sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="output"}[5m]))

# Alert rule: ratio spikes more than 3x above prior 24-hour baseline
(
  sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="input"}[5m]))
    /
  sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="output"}[5m]))
)
>
3 * (
  sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="input"}[24h]))
    /
  sum(rate(genai_chat_completion_tokens_total{token_type="output"}[24h]))
)
Daily and Monthly Spend Forecast

A spend forecast computed from the current daily rate lets finance teams act before a monthly budget is exhausted. The forecast is simple: the current rate of spend extrapolated over the remaining days in the billing period.

from datetime import date


def forecast_monthly_spend(
    current_spend_usd: float,
    as_of_day: int,           # day of month (1–31)
    days_in_month: int = 30,
) -> dict:
    days_remaining = days_in_month - as_of_day
    daily_rate = current_spend_usd / as_of_day
    projected_total = current_spend_usd + daily_rate * days_remaining

    return {
        "current_spend_usd": current_spend_usd,
        "daily_rate_usd": daily_rate,
        "projected_month_total_usd": projected_total,
        "days_remaining": days_remaining,
    }

# Monthly spend forecast in USD
# Current month accumulated + remaining days at today's rate
sum(increase(genai_cost_usd_total[30d]))
+ (
  sum(rate(genai_cost_usd_total[1d])) * (30 - day_of_month())
)
Cost Attribution Across Teams and Features

Without attribution tags, cost spikes cannot be routed to a responsible team. The cost attribution model requires that every cost metric carries four tag dimensions: provider (which vendor), team (which engineering squad), feature (which product capability), and tier (which user class). These four dimensions support the three questions teams ask about GenAI cost: who is spending it, on what feature, and for which customers.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class CostAttributionReport:
    period: str
    total_usd: float
    by_team: dict[str, float]
    by_feature: dict[str, float]
    by_model: dict[str, float]
    by_tier: dict[str, float]

    def top_spenders(self, n: int = 5) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        return sorted(self.by_team.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:n]

    def efficiency_report(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"Cost Attribution — {self.period}"]
        lines.append(f"  Total: ${self.total_usd:.2f}")
        lines.append("  Top teams by spend:")
        for team, spend in self.top_spenders():
            pct = spend / self.total_usd * 100
            lines.append(f"    {team}: ${spend:.2f} ({pct:.1f}%)")
        lines.append("  By model:")
        for model, spend in sorted(self.by_model.items(),
                                   key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
            lines.append(f"    {model}: ${spend:.2f}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Cost breakdown by team (30-day window)
sum by (team) (
  increase(genai_cost_usd_total{env="production"}[30d])
)

# Cost breakdown by feature
sum by (feature) (
  increase(genai_cost_usd_total{env="production"}[30d])
)
Budget Alerts and Automatic Throttling

A cost budget without an enforcement mechanism is a target, not a constraint. Production GenAI systems need two cost controls: an alert that fires before the budget is exhausted so a human can act, and an automatic throttling mechanism that degrades gracefully when the budget is exceeded rather than generating unbounded spend.
Token Efficiency Optimisation Signals
Token and cost metrics reveal where engineering effort on efficiency will have the highest ROI. The following derived metrics point at specific optimisation opportunities.
Metric
What it reveals
Optimisation action
High input:output ratio
System prompt or RAG context is large relative to completions
Audit prompt templates; reduce RAG top-k; add context compression
Rising cost per request at flat traffic
Average request is getting more expensive — longer prompts or model upgrade
Compare prompt token distribution before/after last deploy
Cache hit rate below 40 %
Repeated identical prompts are not hitting the cache
Check cache key logic; ensure deterministic prompt templating
Context utilisation above 80 % at p95
Approaching context window limits; truncation risk
Add summarisation step for long contexts; use sliding window
Output token variance very high
LLM is generating inconsistent response lengths
Add explicit length instruction to system prompt; check temperature
Summary

Token usage and cost are operational signals that require the same real-time monitoring infrastructure as latency and error rates. The foundation is accurate token counting from provider response objects, tagged with the four attribution dimensions: provider, team, feature, and tier. Derived signals — cost per output token, input-to-output ratio, daily spend rate, and monthly forecast — convert raw counters into actionable indicators. Budget alerts and automatic throttling enforce cost constraints gracefully rather than generating unbounded spend. Token efficiency metrics (context utilisation, cache hit rate, ratio anomalies) point directly at the engineering optimisations with the highest cost reduction ROI.
